content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

November 30, 1976


The Honorable Gary G. Gillmor
Mayor of Santa Clara
1500 Warburton Avenue
Santa Clara, CA 95050

Re: Opinion Request No. 76-089

Dear Mayor Gillmor:

Thank you for your letter of November 9, 1976, re-
questing an opinion with regard to whether you are precluded
from participating in certain decisions of the City Council
relative to a proposed high-rise structure due to a conflict
of interest within the meaning of the Political Reform Act.
This letter is to inform you that the Commission will issue
an opinion in response to the question you have asked.

The Commission reserves the right to request addi-
tional information which the Commission or its staff deems
relevant to the preparation of the opinion. A draft opinion
will be prepared by the staff for presentation to the Commis-
sion and a copy of this proposed draft will be forwarded to
you as soon as it is available.

Based on your telephone conversation with Ed Kerry,
a member of the Commission's staff, it is my understanding
that you plan to refrain from participating in decisions con-
cerning the high-rise structure pending the issuance of the
Commission's opinion. In light of the District Attorney's con-
clusion in his letter of November 5, 1976, that you do have a
conflict of interest within the meaning of the Political Reform
Act, we think that this course of action is appropriate and our
interim advice (pending issuance of the opinion) is that you
not participate in decisions concerning the high-rise structure.

If you have any questions, please contact Kenneth
Finney, Chief of the Commission's Legal Division.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:KF:jo

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5591 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

GARY G. GILLMOR
MAYOR

THE CITY OF SANTA CLARA
CALIFORNIA

1500 WARBURTON AV
684-3250

November 9, 1976

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Commission Members:

Presently, within our community, there is a controversy regarding a
proposed development which includes a nine-story structure with 176
one-bedroom units for senior citizen housing, and related facilities
on the first floor, plus 6,400 square feet for rental to small com-
mercial shops. A commercial plaza is planned for the northwest
corner of the property, bordering on Jackson Street. I am the owner
of a portion of the Franklin Mall commercial complex which is across
Jackson Street from the planned development, and I have disclosed
this fact in my publicly filed Statement of Economic Interest accord-
ing to the law.

The City Council had approved in concept the building of a senior
citizen high-rise complex with associated commercial activities on
the bottom floor for this site in previous actions; however, when the
issue actually came before the City Council, the Council, for politi-
cal and other reasons, was split on a three to three vote with one
abstention. I had abstained on some occasions because I was not
clear in my own mind whether or not I could vote because of my
proximity to the project, and I was going to rely on our City Attor-
ney's opinion. I did not pursue the matter to its fullest extent
because there seemed to be unanimity on the Council for the project,
and my vote was not necessary, even though I strongly favored housing
for the elderly in need. I also abstained on some votes on this
matter stating reasons for the record.

Now it seems my vote is very critical to the project and, therefore,
people who differ with my opinion have gone to the District Attorney
in an attempt to nullify my right to vote and represent people of
other persuasions for whom I was elected. Enclosed is an opinion
from the Santa Clara County District Attorney's office as to their
feelings on my right to vote. I differ with them vehemently and

Fair Political Practices Commission                    November 9, 1976
                                                                 Page 2

particularly their unsupported conclusions "that construction of the
proposed development would have a material effect upon one or more
of my financial interests..." and also based upon their inaccurate
investigation and fact finding.  They acknowledge that no expert
opinion was obtained in this regard although the County has an
inhouse staff of professional appraisers.  Their opinion is contrary
to my own opinions and experience.  Since this is a very important
decision for the people of Santa Clara and the senior citizens, I
need an immediate answer to this particular problem, as well as the
more profound implications that the District Attorney seems to
conclude as a result of his miniature investigation.

These are the facts as I see them as compared to the District Attorney's office.

     1.   To my knowledge, I have no financial interest in nor have
I ever received any money from business involvement with the
Real Tech organization (the builders of the project) or any
parent organization.

     2.   The Santa Clara Methodist Retirement Foundation, Inc. (the
owner of the project) is a non-profit organization with church
affiliation.  I am not a member of their church nor of any of
their non-profit organizations, and I have no bias or involvement in their activities.

     3.   I do not own the bar as stated in the District Attorney's
letter of November 5, 1976, and it is apparent that his investigation was lacking in substance.

     4.   When I purchased the buildings in the early 70's, it had
been planned for years that the urban renewal area, where the
retirement project is planned, was to be commercial.  I had
this in mind when I purchased the buildings.  This is the downtown section of Santa Clara and it was no private information,
but very public, that it is a commercial area and will continue
to be a commercial area.  As a matter of fact this, in my opinion, is somewhat of a downgrading of the commercial area;
however, I believe it to be of strong social significance to
help those senior citizens who cannot afford adequate housing.

     5.   None of my leases have a percentage clause; therefore, I
see no commercial advantage to having the seniors live in the

Fair Political Practices Commission                                November 9, 1976
                                                                           Page 3

area because they are going to have their own eating facilities
as well as their own restaurant in this complex.

     6.     It is a fact that I own my real estate business; however,
my records will show that I have never received any real estate
business from the Methodist Foundation, Real Tech, or any
senior citizen that presently lives in the existing Methodist
Liberty Tower project, which is across the street from the
above proposed retirement project, and I do not anticipate any
real estate business from these people.  Generally, these
people are federally subsidized and cannot qualify for real
estate loans; they are retired and not looking for investments.

     7.     The proposed multi-story development, which is supposed
to have a "material financial effect" upon one or more of my
financial interests in Franklin Mall, is quite similar to the
existing Methodist Liberty Tower project, which is across the
street from the newly proposed retirement project.  I have
reviewed the Factors under 2 Administrative Code Section 18702
which your commission has established as a guide in determining
"material financial effect."  Although difficulties arise as
to objective proof of a future effect; I have concluded that
the decision would not materially affect any of my financial
interests.  One of the factors which I consider supportive
of this conclusion is that I have reviewed my records and find
that there was no discernible effect on my financial relation-
ships or interests when the first Liberty Tower project was
approved, constructed, and occupied.  The major difference
between the two projects, in my eyes, is that the newly proposed
project is generally about 100 feet closer to my property and
financial interests cited in the District Attorney's letter
than is the Liberty Towers project, which had no discernible
effect on my financial interests.

     8.     The paramount issue is the fact that no small businessman
who owns a real estate business and makes over $10,000 a year
can serve as a public official.  The District Attorney (page
two, item 4 of his letter) states that this is one reason I
cannot vote on this project.  If this is the case, I would be
precluded from voting on any major development in Santa Clara
because it might affect my income and, therefore, my vote would
be subject to criminal action.  I am convinced that I can
perform my duties as Mayor in this situation in an impartial

Fair Political Practices Commission                                November 9, 1976
                                                                                                                                    Page 4

     manner, free from bias. To me this is unconstitutional and
unfair, because I was elected to bring about a more solid tax
base and progress for the community; if I cannot vote on major
issues, I cannot fulfill my responsibilities.

In summary, it must be stated that I have no financial interest in
this project. I purchased my buildings with full knowledge that the
proposed area was to be commercial and anticipated this future
growth on my buildings. There is no reason to believe that retired
individuals on federal subsidies are going to enhance my commercial,
industrial, or residential real estate business. Therefore, I be-
lieve I should be eligible to vote on this project. It is important
to the City's growth as well as to the needy seniors. If you have
any questions, please advise.

                                                                    Sincerely,

                                                                    [signature]
                                                                    Gary G. Gillmor
                                                                    Mayor
                                                                    City of Santa Clara

GGG/db
Enclosures

sections.conclusion: Based on your telephone conversation with Ed Kerry, a member of the Commission's staff, it is my understanding that you plan to refrain from participating in decisions concerning the high-rise structure pending the issuance of the Commission's opinion. In light of the District Attorney's conclusion in his letter of November 5, 1976, that you do have a conflict of interest within the meaning of the Political Reform Act, We think that this course of action is appropriate and our interim advice (pending issuance of the opinion) is that you not participate in decisions concerning the high-rise structure.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Mayor Gary G. Gillmor has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act that would preclude him from participating in City Council decisions regarding a proposed nine-story senior citizen housing complex development, given his ownership of commercial property across the street from the development site.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises Mayor Gillmor that, pending issuance of a formal opinion, he should refrain from participating in decisions concerning the high-rise structure based on the District Attorney's conclusion that a conflict of interest exists under the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the FPPC response letter (dated November 30, 1976) and the Mayor's incoming request letter (dated November 9, 1976). The FPPC letter is brief and indicates a formal opinion will be forthcoming. The Mayor's letter provides detailed factual background and arguments regarding why he believes he has no conflict of interest. Document quality is poor (appears to be a photocopy with scanning artifacts), but key content is legible.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Mayor Gary G. Gillmor has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act that would preclude him from participating in City Council decisions regarding a proposed nine-story senior citizen housing complex development, given his ownership of commercial property across the street from the development site.

CONCLUSION: Based on your telephone conversation with Ed Kerry, a member of the Commission's staff, it is my understanding that you plan to refrain from participating in decisions concerning the high-rise structure pending the issuance of the Commission's opinion. In light of the District Attorney's conclusion in his letter of November 5, 1976, that you do have a conflict of interest within the meaning of the Political Reform Act, We think that this course of action is appropriate and our interim advice (pending issuance of the opinion) is that you not participate in decisions concerning the high-rise structure.

embedding.summary: This is an interim advice letter from FPPC Executive Director Michael Bennett to Mayor Gary G. Gillmor regarding a conflict of interest question concerning his participation in City Council votes on a proposed senior citizen housing development. The letter provides interim advice that the Mayor refrain from participating in the decision pending a full opinion from the Commission.
